stem: You are the emergency registrar reviewing a 28-year-old non-Aboriginal woman with a four hour history of worsening breathlessness and wheeze on a background of asthma. She has used 12 puffs of salbutamol at home in the last hour without much relief. She is able to speak in short phrases. She has not required steroids in the last six months. She has no penicillin allergy and is up to date with her influenza vaccine.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 °C, HR 116, BP 124/76, RR 26, SpO2 93% room air
data_table.Examination: Bilateral expiratory wheeze, mild accessory muscle use, able to complete short sentences, no cyanosis
data_table.Investigations: Peak expiratory flow 200 L/min (personal best 420 L/min), chest x-ray clear, no fever

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Intramuscular adrenaline 0.5 mg, nebulised ipratropium and oral antibiotics empirically for presumed lower respiratory tract infection driving the exacerbation
options.rationale: Intramuscular adrenaline is reserved for anaphylaxis or near-fatal asthma; routine antibiotics are not indicated without features of bacterial infection. (Source: National Asthma Council Australia Handbook)

options.text: Inhaled fluticasone-formoterol four puffs via spacer and discharge home with an updated written asthma action plan after 30 minutes of observation in the department
options.rationale: Discharge after only 30 minutes with a peak flow of 200 L/min and ongoing distress is unsafe; bronchodilator response must be assessed at one hour and oral corticosteroids initiated. (Source: National Asthma Council Australia Handbook)

options.text: Intravenous hydrocortisone 200 mg, intravenous aminophylline loading infusion and admission to high dependency for continuous cardiac and respiratory monitoring
options.rationale: Intravenous corticosteroid offers no advantage over oral in an alert cooperative patient and aminophylline is reserved for severe disease unresponsive to first-line therapy. (Source: National Asthma Council Australia Handbook)

options.text: Salbutamol 12 puffs via spacer (or 5 mg nebulised) every 20 minutes for one hour, oral prednisolone 50 mg, oxygen titrated to SpO2 of 93 to 95%, and reassess at one hour
options.rationale: Moderate asthma exacerbation is managed with repeated salbutamol via spacer or nebuliser every 20 minutes for an hour, early oral prednisolone 50 mg and oxygen targeted to 93 to 95%; reassessment at one hour decides disposition. (Source: National Asthma Council Australia Handbook)

options.text: Heliox-driven salbutamol nebulisation with immediate intubation if no response, and intravenous magnesium sulfate 10 g infused over 30 minutes as first-line therapy
options.rationale: Heliox and intravenous magnesium are reserved for severe or life-threatening asthma after failure of first-line therapy; high-dose magnesium of 10 g is not the standard adult dose. (Source: National Asthma Council Australia Handbook)

explanation.summary: Moderate adult asthma is graded by ability to speak in phrases, heart rate under 120, oxygen saturation 92 to 94% on air and peak expiratory flow 50 to 75% of personal best or predicted. First-line treatment is salbutamol 12 puffs via spacer (or 5 mg nebulised) every 20 minutes for an hour, with oxygen targeted to 93 to 95% and oral prednisolone 50 mg within the first hour. Ipratropium bromide is added in severe exacerbations. Intravenous magnesium sulfate 2 g over 20 minutes is reserved for severe or life-threatening exacerbations not responding to first-line therapy.
key_points: Salbutamol 12 puffs via spacer (or 5 mg nebulised) every 20 minutes for 1 hour
Oral prednisolone 50 mg early; intravenous hydrocortisone only if unable to swallow
Oxygen titrated to SpO2 93 to 95%, not 100%
Ipratropium added for severe exacerbations
Intravenous magnesium 2 g for severe disease unresponsive to first-line therapy
explanation.pearls: Oxygen target in acute asthma is 93 to 95% saturation because higher saturations risk worsening V/Q mismatch and hypercapnia, mirroring the COPD principle.

stem: You are the medical registrar urgently reviewing a 72-year-old non-Aboriginal man with chronic kidney disease stage 4 brought in by his wife with weakness and nausea. He takes ramipril, spironolactone, metformin and trimethoprim for a recent urinary tract infection. He missed his haemodialysis-free clinic review last week. He has had no chest pain. He has no contraindication to insulin therapy.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.6 °C, HR 48, BP 110/68, RR 18, SpO2 98% room air
data_table.Examination: Alert, normal mental state, no peripheral oedema, soft abdomen
data_table.Investigations: K 7.4 mmol/L, Na 136, urea 23.1, creatinine 358 micromol/L, bicarbonate 18, glucose 6.8; ECG sinus bradycardia 48 with peaked T waves, PR 220 ms, broad QRS 124 ms

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Intravenous sodium polystyrene sulfonate (resonium) 30 g and oral sodium bicarbonate 1 g three times daily while awaiting renal review later today
options.rationale: Resonium is intravenously inappropriate (oral or rectal) and acts slowly over hours; it does not address the immediate cardiac membrane risk from a potassium of 7.4 with ECG changes. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Intravenous frusemide 80 mg, oral salbutamol 8 mg and arrange a fast track to outpatient haemodialysis later today via the renal registrar on call team
options.rationale: Loop diuretic is slow and unlikely to be effective in advanced kidney disease, and oral salbutamol is not used; nebulised salbutamol is one of the membrane and shift agents. (Source: KHA-CARI Hyperkalaemia Guidelines)

options.text: Insulin actrapid 10 units in 50 mL 50% dextrose intravenously and reassess potassium and ECG at 30 minutes without giving any calcium because the patient is haemodynamically stable
options.rationale: ECG changes with severe hyperkalaemia mandate immediate intravenous calcium for cardiac membrane stabilisation before shift therapy; relying on insulin-dextrose alone delays myocardial protection. (Source: KHA-CARI Hyperkalaemia Guidelines)

options.text: Activate the medical emergency team and prepare for intubation given the bradycardia and broad QRS, with definitive cardiac pacing and central venous access before any drug therapy is administered
options.rationale: Drug therapy for hyperkalaemia is faster than mechanical pacing and resolves the underlying cause; intubation is not required if the patient is alert and ventilating. (Source: KHA-CARI Hyperkalaemia Guidelines)

options.text: Intravenous calcium gluconate 10 mL of 10% solution over 5 minutes for cardiac membrane stabilisation, then insulin-dextrose and nebulised salbutamol, and urgent dialysis referral
options.rationale: Severe hyperkalaemia with ECG changes requires immediate calcium gluconate for cardiac membrane stabilisation, then potassium-shifting therapy with insulin-dextrose and salbutamol, and dialysis for definitive removal in this patient with advanced kidney disease. (Source: KHA-CARI Hyperkalaemia Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Severe hyperkalaemia with ECG changes (peaked T waves, broad QRS, sine wave) is a cardiac emergency. Management proceeds in three stages: cardiac membrane stabilisation with intravenous calcium gluconate 10 mL of 10% (or calcium chloride 10 mL of 10% if central access), potassium shift into cells with insulin 10 units in 50 mL of 50% dextrose plus nebulised salbutamol 10 to 20 mg, and potassium elimination via dialysis, frusemide if urine output preserved, or oral resin slowly. Identify and reverse contributors: ACE inhibitors, ARBs, spironolactone, trimethoprim, NSAIDs, missed dialysis.
key_points: Calcium gluconate first for ECG changes; effect within 3 minutes, lasts about 30 minutes
Insulin-dextrose and nebulised salbutamol shift potassium intracellularly over 15 to 30 minutes
Dialysis is definitive for advanced CKD or severe refractory hyperkalaemia
Stop ACE inhibitors, ARBs, spironolactone, trimethoprim and NSAIDs
Repeat ECG and potassium at 30 minutes after intervention
explanation.pearls: Trimethoprim and ACE inhibitor combination in advanced CKD is a common precipitant of severe hyperkalaemia through epithelial sodium channel block plus aldosterone suppression.

stem: You are the medical registrar called urgently to assess a 65-year-old non-Aboriginal woman who has had a witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure on the ward. She was admitted three days ago for an exacerbation of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and given intravenous fluids. She drinks no alcohol and is on no diuretics. She is now post-ictal but rousable. Her serum sodium on admission was 138 mmol/L and is now 116 mmol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.6 °C, HR 92, BP 132/78, RR 18, SpO2 96% on 1 L oxygen via nasal prongs
data_table.Examination: Post-ictal, GCS 12, no focal neurological deficit, soft abdomen, no oedema
data_table.Investigations: Na 116 mmol/L, K 4.0, urea 4.2, creatinine 76 micromol/L, serum osmolality 248, urine osmolality 510, urine sodium 68, TSH normal, cortisol normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: 0.9% sodium chloride 1000 mL infusion over 4 hours and restrict free water intake to under 1 L per 24 hours with daily electrolyte review for 3 days
options.rationale: Isotonic saline alone does not correct seizing severe hyponatraemia and may even worsen sodium in SIADH; hypertonic saline is required for symptomatic severe hyponatraemia. (Source: KHA-CARI Hyponatraemia Guidelines)

options.text: Oral fluid restriction to 800 mL per day combined with oral sodium chloride 1 g three times daily and reassess sodium in 12 hours with intravenous fluids ceased
options.rationale: Oral measures act too slowly for severe symptomatic hyponatraemia with seizure; hypertonic saline is required to raise sodium acutely. (Source: KHA-CARI Hyponatraemia Guidelines)

options.text: Demeclocycline 300 mg twice daily orally for one week and continue current intravenous fluids and supportive care without active sodium correction in the next 6 hours
options.rationale: Demeclocycline is occasionally used in chronic SIADH for outpatients and has no role in acute symptomatic severe hyponatraemia with seizure. (Source: KHA-CARI Hyponatraemia Guidelines)

options.text: Oral tolvaptan 15 mg single dose now with serial sodium measurements at 2 hours and admission to high dependency for cardiac and respiratory monitoring
options.rationale: Tolvaptan can cause uncontrolled rapid correction and is contraindicated in acute symptomatic severe hyponatraemia; hypertonic saline is preferred. (Source: KHA-CARI Hyponatraemia Guidelines)

options.text: Intravenous 3% sodium chloride 150 mL bolus over 10 minutes, repeat to a maximum of three boluses, with sodium target rise of 4 to 6 mmol/L in the first hour and capped at 10 mmol/L per 24 hours
options.rationale: Severe symptomatic hyponatraemia with seizure requires immediate hypertonic saline boluses targeting a 4 to 6 mmol/L rise in the first hour, capped at 10 mmol/L per 24 hours to prevent osmotic demyelination. (Source: KHA-CARI Hyponatraemia Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Severe symptomatic hyponatraemia (seizure, reduced conscious state, vomiting from cerebral oedema) is a neurological emergency. Management is 3% sodium chloride 150 mL intravenous bolus over 10 minutes, repeated up to three times until symptoms resolve, aiming for a 4 to 6 mmol/L rise in the first hour. Total correction must not exceed 10 mmol/L in 24 hours, or 18 mmol/L in 48 hours, to avoid osmotic demyelination syndrome. Subsequent management addresses underlying cause: SIADH from drugs, malignancy, central nervous system or pulmonary disease; volume depletion with isotonic saline; or hypervolaemia with diuresis.
key_points: Severe symptomatic hyponatraemia mandates 3% saline 150 mL boluses
Target rise 4 to 6 mmol/L in first hour to resolve symptoms
Cap correction at 10 mmol/L in 24 hours, 18 mmol/L in 48 hours
Recheck sodium every 2 hours during active correction
Identify cause: drugs, SIADH triggers, volume status
explanation.pearls: If correction exceeds 10 mmol/L in 24 hours, give 5% dextrose 6 mL/kg over 1 to 2 hours to relower sodium and prevent osmotic demyelination.

subtopic_detail: Acute on chronic respiratory acidosis interpretation
stem: You are the respiratory registrar reviewing a 70-year-old non-Aboriginal man with a 60 pack-year smoking history presenting with three days of worsening breathlessness and increased sputum production. He has known severe chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and was given controlled oxygen by the paramedics. He is drowsy but rousable to voice. He has been on home oxygen 1 L overnight only. He has no chest pain.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.4 °C, HR 110, BP 138/82, RR 26, SpO2 92% on 28% Venturi mask
data_table.Examination: Pursed lip breathing, accessory muscle use, asterixis, expiratory wheeze and prolonged expiratory phase, no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: ABG on 28%: pH 7.28, paCO2 70, paO2 62, HCO3 32, base excess +6; chest x-ray hyperinflated with no infiltrate; CRP 42, WCC 12.4

lead_in: Which interpretation of the arterial blood gas is most accurate?

options.text: Acute respiratory acidosis with normal bicarbonate consistent with a primary new pulmonary embolism rather than a chronic obstructive pulmonary disease exacerbation today
options.rationale: The bicarbonate is elevated at 32 indicating metabolic compensation present, not acute respiratory acidosis alone; the picture fits acute on chronic respiratory acidosis. (Source: TSANZ COPD Guidelines)

options.text: Compensated chronic respiratory acidosis at baseline because the pH is only mildly low and the bicarbonate is elevated, requiring no escalation in current ventilation support
options.rationale: If the picture were fully compensated chronic respiratory acidosis the pH would be near normal; pH 7.28 with paCO2 70 indicates acute decompensation on top of chronic retention. (Source: TSANZ COPD Guidelines)

options.text: Primary metabolic alkalosis with secondary hyperventilation as compensation, suggesting overuse of bicarbonate-containing antacids or recent vomiting as the dominant disturbance
options.rationale: Metabolic alkalosis would raise pH, not lower it; the low pH and very high paCO2 indicate respiratory acidosis as the primary disturbance. (Source: TSANZ COPD Guidelines)

options.text: Hyperoxic hypercapnia from oxygen toxicity alone, which would correct with cessation of supplemental oxygen and switching to room air without ventilation support
options.rationale: Stopping oxygen risks fatal hypoxia; the pattern reflects acute on chronic ventilatory failure requiring non-invasive ventilation, with oxygen titrated to 88 to 92%. (Source: TSANZ COPD Guidelines)

options.text: Acute on chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation; non-invasive bilevel ventilation is indicated and oxygen should be titrated to a target saturation of 88 to 92%
options.rationale: Elevated bicarbonate indicates chronic CO2 retention with renal compensation, while the acidaemia and very high paCO2 indicate acute decompensation; non-invasive ventilation is indicated and oxygen targeted to 88 to 92% prevents worsening hypercapnia. (Source: TSANZ COPD Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Acute on chronic respiratory acidosis is characterised by acidaemia, raised paCO2 above the chronic baseline and an elevated bicarbonate reflecting prior renal compensation. In COPD exacerbation with pH under 7.35 and paCO2 over 45 despite controlled oxygen and bronchodilator therapy, non-invasive bilevel ventilation is the standard of care because it reduces intubation, mortality and hospital length of stay. Oxygen is titrated to a target saturation of 88 to 92% to avoid worsening hypercapnia from loss of hypoxic drive and reversal of pulmonary vasoconstriction.
key_points: Elevated HCO3 plus acidaemia signals acute on chronic respiratory acidosis
NIV indicated for pH under 7.35 and paCO2 over 45 despite optimal medical therapy
Oxygen target 88 to 92% in COPD to avoid hypercapnia
Bronchodilators, prednisolone 30 to 50 mg orally for 5 days and antibiotic if 2 of 3 Anthonisen criteria
Failure of NIV with worsening pH or conscious state prompts invasive ventilation
explanation.pearls: Bilevel non-invasive ventilation typically starts at IPAP 12 and EPAP 5 with FiO2 titrated to SpO2 88 to 92%, with arterial blood gas reassessed at 1 hour.

stem: You are the emergency registrar reviewing a 56-year-old non-Aboriginal man with new severe headache and blurred vision for 6 hours. He has hypertension treated with poorly compliant amlodipine 10 mg daily. He has had no chest pain and no focal limb weakness. He is alert and oriented. His urinalysis is positive for protein and blood. Fundoscopy reveals flame haemorrhages and cotton wool spots.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 °C, HR 88, BP 224/132, RR 18, SpO2 98% room air
data_table.Examination: Alert, no focal neurological deficit, no carotid bruit, chest clear, no peripheral oedema, fundoscopy with flame haemorrhages and cotton wool spots
data_table.Investigations: Creatinine 142 micromol/L (baseline 88), urea 9.4, urinalysis protein 2+ blood 1+, troponin normal, ECG sinus rhythm with voltage criteria for LV hypertrophy, CT brain no haemorrhage

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial blood pressure management?

options.text: Oral captopril 25 mg sublingually with target blood pressure under 140/90 in the first hour and discharge home once below target on the same antihypertensive regimen
options.rationale: Sublingual captopril produces rapid uncontrolled drops in pressure and the target of under 140/90 in the first hour risks cerebral hypoperfusion; admission with parenteral therapy is required. (Source: NHFA Hypertension Guidelines)

options.text: Sublingual nifedipine 10 mg every 15 minutes until pressure is below 140/90, then commence oral perindopril and arrange general practice review the following week
options.rationale: Sublingual nifedipine causes uncontrolled drops in blood pressure with risk of stroke and myocardial ischaemia and is no longer recommended in any hypertensive presentation. (Source: NHFA Hypertension Guidelines)

options.text: Intravenous frusemide 80 mg bolus and oral metoprolol 100 mg with target pressure under 140/90 in one hour and admission to the general medical ward for observation
options.rationale: Frusemide and oral therapy do not provide the controlled, titrable reduction needed for hypertensive emergency; intravenous infusion of a short-acting agent in a monitored bed is preferred. (Source: NHFA Hypertension Guidelines)

options.text: Increase his amlodipine to 20 mg daily and add irbesartan 300 mg orally with home blood pressure monitoring; arrange cardiology outpatient review within 4 weeks
options.rationale: End-organ damage (acute kidney injury, retinopathy) makes this a hypertensive emergency requiring inpatient parenteral therapy, not titration of oral medications at home. (Source: NHFA Hypertension Guidelines)

options.text: Intravenous glyceryl trinitrate infusion titrated in a monitored bed targeting a mean arterial pressure reduction of 20 to 25% in the first hour, then to 160/100 over 6 hours
options.rationale: Hypertensive emergency with end-organ damage is treated with a titrated intravenous infusion (GTN or labetalol) in a monitored bed, with mean arterial pressure reduced by 20 to 25% in the first hour, then to 160/100 over 2 to 6 hours, to avoid hypoperfusion. (Source: NHFA Hypertension Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Hypertensive emergency is severe hypertension (typically over 180/120) with evidence of acute end-organ damage (encephalopathy, retinopathy, acute kidney injury, pulmonary oedema, aortic dissection or eclampsia). It requires immediate admission to a monitored bed and titrated intravenous antihypertensive therapy. The pressure should be lowered by 20 to 25% in the first hour, then to 160/100 over the next 2 to 6 hours; faster reduction risks watershed cerebral, renal or coronary infarction. Specific scenarios alter agent choice: labetalol or esmolol with sodium nitroprusside for aortic dissection, GTN for pulmonary oedema, magnesium for eclampsia.
key_points: Hypertensive emergency requires end-organ damage, not just BP above 180/120
Lower mean arterial pressure by 20 to 25% in the first hour, no faster
Then aim for 160/100 over 2 to 6 hours
Intravenous GTN or labetalol in a monitored bed
Sublingual nifedipine and captopril are contraindicated
explanation.pearls: Aortic dissection requires aggressive beta-blockade with labetalol or esmolol first to reduce shear stress before vasodilator therapy, with target systolic 100 to 120 mmHg and heart rate under 60.
